attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a slowly enlarging lesion on her ear that has been present for six months. She migrated from Nepal one year ago. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She denies fever, weight loss, or night sweats.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a well-defined, reddish-brown nodule on her left ear measuring 1.5 cm in diameter. The plaque has a soft, gelatinous consistency. When pressed with a glass slide, the lesion demonstrates an apple jelly-like appearance. The remainder of her examination, including lymph node assessment, is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of cutaneous tuberculosis (TB), specifically lupus vulgaris. The characteristic finding is the apple jelly appearance on diascopy (pressing with a glass slide), which is pathognomonic. The patient's background, being from a tuberculosis-endemic region, adds support to the diagnosis as patients are usually highly sensitised to TB. It typically presents as a soft, reddish-brown plaque that grows slowly and peripherally. The face and head is the most commonly affected site, though it also tends to grow in previous scars.

Discoid lupus erythematosus typically presents with erythematous plaques with adherent scaling and follicular plugging. While it can affect the face, it lacks the characteristic apple jelly appearance on diascopy and tends to show atrophy and scarring.

Sarcoidosis can present with similar-appearing plaques, but these typically appear more firm and violaceous. While diascopy may reveal a similar appearance, other features of sarcoidosis such as bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy are often present.

Leishmaniasis typically begins as a papule that evolves into an ulcer with raised borders. The history would usually include travel to endemic areas within the past few months, and the progression would be more rapid.

Basal cell carcinoma typically presents as a pearly papule or nodule with telangiectasia. While it can occur on the face, it lacks the characteristic apple jelly appearance on diascopy and usually has a different texture.

Diagnosis of lupus vulgaris should be confirmed with skin biopsy for histopathology and culture. Tuberculin skin test is usually strongly positive. Treatment involves standard anti-tuberculous therapy for 6-12 months.
learningPoints: The characteristic 'apple jelly' appearance on diascopy is pathognomonic for lupus vulgaris.
Diagnosis of cutaneous tuberculosis requires correlation of clinical features with histopathology and culture.
references: Hill MK, Sanders CV. Cutaneous Tuberculosis. Microbiology Spectrum. 2017.  Available at: https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/microbiolspec.tnmi7-0010-2016

Santos JB, Figueiredo AR, Ferraz CE, Oliveira MH, Silva PG, Medeiros VL. Cutaneous tuberculosis: epidemiologic, etiopathogenic and clinical aspects - part I. Anais Brasileiros de Dermatolgia. 2014. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4008050/
Ngan V, Oakley A. Cutaneous tuberculosis. DermNet NZ. 2023. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/cutaneous-tuberculosis
